title: Normal and Abnormal Brain Development
authors: Gressens P, Hüppi PS / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเข้าใจ Normal and Abnormal Brain Development?
สมองทารกไม่ใช่สมองผู้ใหญ่ขนาดเล็ก — มันกำลัง build itself ขณะที่เราดูแลทารกอยู่ใน NICU. (1) สมองพัฒนาผ่าน 10 ขั้นตอนที่ต่อเนื่องกัน ตั้งแต่ gestational week 3 จนถึง 2–3 ปีหลังคลอด — แต่ละขั้นมี critical window ที่ vulnerable ต่อ insult; (2) Brain malformation ส่วนใหญ่ reflect TIMING ของ injury ไม่ใช่ NATURE ของ injury — เข้าใจ timeline → เข้าใจ phenotype; (3) Preoligodendrocyte เป็น dominant cell ใน periventricular white matter ช่วง 23–32 wk → vulnerable ต่อ oxidative stress, excitotoxicity, HI → นี่คือ mechanistic basis ของ periventricular leukomalacia (PVL); (4) Drugs ที่เราใช้ประจำ — phenobarbital, ketamine, benzos — block NMDA/activate GABA-A → exacerbate physiologic apoptosis ใน developing brain (Olney 2002); (5) Kangaroo care ช่วยลบล้าง HPA axis damage จาก painful NICU procedures (Mooney-Leber Neuroscience 2017). เราต้องรู้ว่าสมองทำอะไรอยู่ เพื่อปกป้องมัน.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
สรุป chronology ของ 10 ขั้นตอนหลักของ brain development ตั้งแต่ neuroectoderm induction ถึง myelination พร้อม gestational week ที่สำคัญ
อธิบาย neurulation: 4 ขั้น, การปิดของ anterior/posterior neuropore, และ failure ของแต่ละส่วน → neural tube defects ชนิดใด
อธิบาย neuronal proliferation: VZ vs SVZ, radial glial cells (aRGC + bRGC), intermediate progenitors — ความแตกต่าง mouse vs human cortex
อธิบาย neuronal migration (12–24 wk): inside-out pattern, radial vs tangential migration, และโมเลกุลที่ควบคุม (LIS1, DCX, Reelin, FilaminA)
อธิบาย subplate neurons: function (guide axons, thalamo-cortical waiting zone), timing (max 22–36 wk), vulnerability ใน preterm WMI
อธิบาย synaptogenesis (5 phases), programmed cell death (15–50% neurons, peak 28–41 wk), และ effect ของ drugs บน apoptosis
อธิบาย oligodendrocyte lineage: 4 stages, vulnerability ของ preoligodendrocytes (23–32 wk), NAA เป็น marker ก่อน myelination
ระบุ myelination sequence จาก Table 53.2: sensory first, motor follows; T1 precedes T2; complete 2–3 yr
วินิจฉัย neural tube defects (anencephaly, MMC, encephalocele) และ prosencephalic malformations (holoprosencephaly, CC agenesis, Dandy-Walker) ในทารกแรกเกิด
จำแนก lissencephalies: type I (LIS1, DCX, tubulin, ARX — gradient, thickness) vs type II cobblestone (WWS, MEB, Fukuyama — O-glycosylation)
ระบุ management ของ myelomeningocele ใน delivery room: surgical closure within 24h, shunt for hydrocephalus
อธิบาย DTI/MRI ในการ assess brain maturation: cortical anisotropy, sulcation index, myelination signal changes
1. Chronology ของ Brain Development
1.1 10 ขั้นตอนหลัก

rows: Organizing centers → inductive molecules (follistatin, noggin, Notch, SHH, Wnt1)
Neural plate → tube; anterior neuropore closes ~day 24; posterior ~day 26
VZ, SVZ (ISVZ + OSVZ); aRGC, bRGC, IPC; unique human OSVZ expansion
Inside-out pattern; radial + tangential; LIS1, DCX, Reelin, FilaminA
15–50% neurons eliminated; caspase cascade; peak in 3rd trimester
5 phases; 40,000 synapses/sec (3rd phase); glutamate/NMDA → BDNF stabilization
Astrocytes (dual origin); OL lineage; preoligodendrocytes peak GW 23–32
Sensory first; T1 shortening before T2; myelination ends in association cortex adulthood
Radial then horizontal; no muscularization until late gestation → ↑ IVH/WMI risk

blocks.text: 1.2 Cortical Growth Milestones ใน Preterm Period
28–40 wk: overall brain volume doubles; cortical gray matter volume ↑ 4-fold; cortical surface area ↑ 5-fold (Hüppi et al Ann Neurol 1998)
Primary sulci: formed by 28 wk (except occipital); secondary + tertiary sulci: 28–40 wk onward
Sulcation index: highest in central region → occipitoparietal → lowest in frontal (medial folds before lateral; central before occipitorostral direction)
Sex difference: females have smaller cortical surface + volumes vs males but equivalent sulcation (Dubois et al Cereb Cortex 2008)
Prematurity: preterm at term equivalent age → ↓ cortical volume + surface, less complex sulcation vs term controls (Inder et al Pediatrics 2005); ↑ effect with ↓ GA at birth
2. Neurulation และ Patterning
2.1 Neurulation (GW 3–4)
4 steps: (1) neural plate formation → (2) neural plate modeling → (3) neural groove formation → (4) neural groove closure → neural tube
Anterior neuropore closes ~day 24; posterior neuropore closes ~day 26 (lumbosacral level); more caudal spinal cord by canalization GW 4–7 + retrogressive differentiation GW 7→postnatal
Failure → neural tube defects; timing → phenotype
CNS regionalization: rostrocaudal (HOX, FGF8, EN, WNT, OTX, EMX, DLX) × dorsoventral (BMP, PAX, SHH) → grid pattern
2.2 Neuronal Proliferation ใน Human Neocortex
Mouse: VZ (aRGC) + SVZ (IPC only) — neurogenesis takes 6 days
Human: VZ + ISVZ + OSVZ (outer SVZ) — เพิ่ม bRGCs (basal RGCs, PAX6+, no apical attachment) + abundant IPCs; neurogenesis takes ~10 wk
bRGCs: largest population 11–17 wk; both symmetric (self-renewal) + asymmetric divisions → IPCs or neurons; major driver of cortical expansion + gyrification in humans
Glutamatergic neurons ← VZ/SVZ; GABAergic interneurons ← ganglionic eminence (tangential migration)
3. Neuronal Migration และ Cortical Lamination
3.1 Inside-Out Pattern (GW 12–24)
First neurons → subpial preplate; subsequent neurons split preplate → molecular layer (layer I, Cajal-Retzius neurons) + deep subplate
Cortical layers II–VI form inside-out: layer VI first, then V, IV, III, II — นั่นคือ neurons ที่เกิดทีหลัง migrate ผ่าน neurons เก่าไปอยู่ชั้นบนกว่า
3.2 Types of Migration
Radial migration: along radial glial guides; nucleokinesis + leading process extension; glutamatergic neurons; Rakic protomap hypothesis
Tangential migration in GZ: initial tangential → then radial along glia; ↑ clonal heterogeneity in cortical plate
Tangential migration in intermediate zone: GABAergic interneurons from ganglionic eminence; cocaine ↓ this pathway specifically
3.3 Molecular Control of Migration

Key Migration Molecules — จำแต่ละ class
1. Cytoskeletal: FilaminA (actin-binding → periventricular heterotopia); DCX/doublecortin (MAP → lissencephaly + double cortex); LIS1 (MAP + dynein regulator → isolated lissencephaly + Miller-Dieker); alpha-tubulin (heterodimer formation). 2. Signaling/Lamination: Reelin (glycoprotein → inverted cortex in Reeler mouse; RELN mutations → lissencephaly + cerebellar hypoplasia in humans); Cdk5/p35 (serine-threonine kinase). 3. Glycosylation (stop signals): POMT1, POMT2, POMGnT1, Fukutin → type II cobblestone lissencephaly (Walker-Warburg, MEB, Fukuyama). 4. Other modulators: glutamate, GABA, BDNF, thyroid hormones, peroxisomal factors, ethanol, cocaine.
DTI of migration: cortical anisotropy max at 26 wk (radial glia direction dominant) → declines to 0 by 36 wk as RGC → astrocyte transformation occurs (McKinstry Cereb Cortex 2002)
4. CNS Organization — Subplate, Synaptogenesis, Apoptosis
4.1 Subplate Neurons
Generated: ~GW 7; subplate generated from preplate ~GW 10
Max thickness: GW 22–36 — present in preterm, ABSENT in term infant (disappear by apoptosis or incorporated into layer VI)
Functions (3 critical ones): (1) pioneer axons for internal capsule; (2) corpus callosum axons GW 25–32; (3)
Thalamo-cortical waiting zone: thalamo-cortical axons synapse on subplate neurons and WAIT before invading cortical plate layer IV → necessary for correct target selection
Vulnerability: periventricular WMI in preterm → destroys subplate → disrupts thalamo-cortical connectivity → cognitive + sensory deficits
MRI visualization: highly hydrophilic extracellular matrix → low T1/slightly high T2 signal at 26 wk on subcortical zone
4.2 Synaptogenesis (5 Phases)
Phase 1 (~GW 6–8): subplate only; experience-independent
Phase 2 (GW 12–17): few cortical synapses on dendritic shafts; experience-independent
Phase 3 (~midgestation – 8 months post-birth): ~40,000 new synapses/second (monkey); partially depends on sensory input
Phase 4 (→ puberty): high rate; highly dependent on sensory experience
Phase 5 (→ adulthood): ongoing; masked by intense synaptic loss
Stabilization mechanism: glutamate → NMDA receptor → Ca²⁺ influx → BDNF production → labile synapse stabilization; nitric oxide also key player
Microglia + synaptic pruning (Tremblay Neuron Glia Biol 2011): quiescent microglia continuously extend processes → phagocytose axon terminals + dendritic spines — essential for circuit refinement
NICU relevance: วิธี stimulation/deprivation ใน preterm period → shapes synaptic network; too-early or inappropriate stimuli → clinical question unresolved
4.3 Programmed Cell Death (Apoptosis)
15–50% ของ neurons ที่สร้างมา → ถูก eliminate โดย physiologic apoptosis
Timing: ~70% ของ neurons ที่ต้องตาย → ตายช่วง GW 28–41 (third trimester = peak apoptotic window)
Pathway: intrinsic (mitochondrial; cytochrome C → caspases; controlled by Bcl-2 family); extrinsic (death receptors, TNF superfamily); caspase-independent (AIF)

Drugs ที่ขัดขวาง Electrical Activity → Exacerbate Apoptosis (Olney 2002)
NMDA blockers: MK-801, ketamine — block electrical activity → ↑ neuronal death. GABA-A agonists: phenobarbital, phenytoin, diazepam, clonazepam, vigabatrin, valproic acid. Anesthetics: midazolam + nitrous oxide + isoflurane combination. Ethanol: blocks NMDA + activates GABA-A → mimics drug effects. Dexamethasone + sulfite excipient: systemic injection in newborn mice → ↑ neuronal death in neocortex + basal ganglia. Clinical implication: postnatal systemic steroids → ↓ cortical gray matter volume (Murphy et al Pediatrics 2001) → ↑ CP + cognitive impairment in follow-up; use ONLY when risk of CLD > risk of neurotoxicity.
5. Gliogenesis และ Myelination
5.1 Astrocytes
Dual origin: (1) transformation of RGCs → astrocytes in deep layers + white matter (peak GW 26–28); (2) migration from periventricular SVZ → superficial cortical layers
Functions: axonal guidance, ↑ neuron survival, synaptic formation, glutamate uptake (prevent excitotoxicity), lactate production (energy for neurons), myelination support, blood-brain barrier

1H-MRS markers: myoinositol (↑ in preterm, ↓ first year); glutamate/glutamine (glx peak term); N-acetylaspartate (NAA; ↑ GW 32–40 → lipid synthesis before myelination); creatine (astrocyte-synthesized)
Low-intensity periventricular bands on T2-MRI <30 wk = normal migrating RGCs (not WMI)
5.2 Oligodendrocyte Lineage

rows: MOST VULNERABLE: oxidative stress, AMPA-kainate, NMDA, HI → PVL mechanism
Highly multipolar; produces myelin; up to 50% undergo apoptosis during development

blocks.text: NAA (N-acetylaspartate): ↑ markedly GW 32–40 in central white matter (Kreis et al Magn Reson Med 2002) → acetyl groups for lipid synthesis → precedes myelination; stable in perithalamic voxels (already myelinating at 32 wk)

Vulnerability: up to 50% OLs undergo apoptosis during development; preoligodendrocytes specifically ↑ vulnerable to: free radical attack (less antioxidant defense), AMPA-kainate receptor excitotoxicity, NMDA receptor activation, HI
5.3 Myelination Sequence

Myelination — Sequence and MRI Timing
General rule: structures that FUNCTION FIRST are MYELINATED FIRST. Sensory before motor; subcortical before cortical; cerebellar pedunculi/vermis before hemispheres. Projection fibers before association fibers. Association fibers complete in adulthood. T1-weighted (lipid → high signal): leads T2. T1 shortening = cholesterol + glycolipid accumulation (preparation). T2 shortening = tightening of myelin spiral + conformational changes in myelin proteins. Key landmarks: VPL thalamus: T1 at 28–30 wk; posterior PLIC: T1 38–40 wk, T2 at term; anterior PLIC: T1 40–48 wk; splenium CC: T1 38–56 wk; genu CC: T1 48–53 wk; frontal WM central T1 52–60 wk. Forebrain myelination starts at birth, largely complete 2–3 yr; association cortex last (adulthood). Myelinated white matter <5% total brain volume at 34 wk → rapid 5-fold increase after 34 wk.
5.4 Microglia
5–15% ของ total cerebral cells; origin: bone marrow precursors (circulating → penetrate brain)
1st trimester: amoeboid morphology; midgestation: mostly in white matter pathways (external/internal capsule, CC)
Normal function: phagocytosis of apoptotic fragments, elimination of exuberant axons/dendrites, synaptic pruning
In injury: transform into brain macrophages → ↑ free radicals + nitric oxide → neurotoxic
5.5 Brain Vascular Development
Neural tube forms → vessels penetrate neuroepithelium immediately; density low during neuronal migration
Radial + ventriculopetal penetration from leptomeningeal plexus; horizontal collaterals appear ~GW 20 (subplate first, layer II–I only postnatally)

No muscularis in neopallial arteries until late gestation; muscularis develops with ventriculopetal gradient → explains lack of vasomotor autoregulation in periventricular areas during 2nd half gestation → germinal matrix hemorrhage + deep WMI susceptibility
6. Disorders — Neural Tube Formation
6.1 Classification by Timing
General principle: morphologic disorder reflects TIMING of noxious event, not its nature

rows: Total failure of neurulation; neural plate-like structure; nearly all spontaneously aborted
Failure anterior NTC; polyhydramnios; 3/4 stillborn; rest die neonatal period
Failure posterior NTC; neural plate replaces spinal cord; mostly stillborn
3/4 occipital; hydrocephalus 50%; lower occipital → Chiari III + CC agenesis
3/4 lumbar; hydrocephalus 70%; Chiari II almost always; close within 24h
Tethered cord (most common), intraspinal lipoma, diastematomyelia; motor/sensory/bladder deficits

blocks.text: 6.2 Myelomeningocele — Neonatal Management
Surgical closure within 24h of birth: prevents further spinal cord/nerve root deterioration + prevents meningitis
Decision factors: size + location; feasibility of closure; prognosis for ambulation; associated abnormalities (hydrocephalus, kyphosis, chromosomal); family wishes
Standard approach: early surgical closure → ventriculoperitoneal shunt if hydrocephalus (present in 70% lumbar MMC)
Long-term: neurosurgical (shunt), orthopedic (ambulation), urologic (UTI prevention, bladder management) follow-up
Etiology: MTHFR mutations × environmental factors (valproate, maternal T1DM); periconceptional folate supplementation ↓ risk
7. Disorders — Prosencephalic Development และ Proliferation
7.1 Holoprosencephaly
Defect in cleavage of prosencephalon — rostrocaudal + transversal axes
Frequency: ~1/10,000 births (including terminations); up to 40/10,000 including embryos

rows: Absent interhemispheric fissure, CC, 3rd ventricle; single ventricle; fused thalami; arhinencephaly; worst prognosis
Interhemispheric fissure present caudally only; frontal horns absent; CC absent
3rd ventricle present; CC present; frontal lobes hypoplastic; continuum with septo-optic dysplasia

blocks.text: Genes: SHH (HPE3, most important); ZIC2 (HPE5); SIX3 (HPE2); TGIF (HPE4); ~12 loci on 11 chromosomes identified
Environmental: maternal diabetes, ethanol; chromosomal: trisomy 13, triploidy; syndromic: Smith-Lemli-Opitz (cholesterol metabolism), Meckel, Pallister-Hall
Neonatal presentation: apneas, seizures, tonic spasms, lack of neurologic development; dysmorphic facies severity ≠ brain severity always
7.2 Corpus Callosum Agenesis
Prevalence: 2–3% ของ intellectual disability population; commonest midline malformation (50%)
Isolated CC agenesis: prognosis variable — some normal/near-normal, some severe; Probst bundles (callosal fibers running longitudinally) = sign of better prognosis
Complete vs partial: partial usually affects posterior portion; with associated malformations → poor prognosis
Associated with: neuronal migration disorders, chromosomal disorders (trisomy 8, 18), syndromes (Aicardi, ACC + chorioretinal lacunae)
7.3 Dandy-Walker Malformation
Abnormal development of rhombencephalon (GW 7–10); 1/25,000–30,000 births; usually sporadic
Classic triad: (1) ↑ posterior fossa + elevated tentorium; (2) cystic dilation 4th ventricle; (3) partial or complete cerebellar vermis agenesis
Hydrocephalus: often present, may appear late (postnatal); other brain malformations in 70% of cases
Differential for large posterior fossa CSF collection: (1) DWM; (2) enlarged cisterna magna; (3) arachnoid cyst — Volpe's 3-category classification
Prognosis: determined largely by associated malformations; isolated DWM has variable prognosis
7.4 Microcephaly
Definition: OFC < −2 SD; severe: < −3 SD for age and sex
Primary vs secondary: primary = genetic (brain never grows normally); secondary = acquired (brain reached expected size then fails to grow)

rows: ASPM (MCPH5), CENPJ (MCPH6); cell-cycle genes; simplified gyral pattern; no cortical dysgenesis; known gene in ~30–50%
Normal/thin cortex; normal or thin CC; agenesis of CC variant; 2 clinical types
THICK cortex; MLIS1 (Norman-Roberts, RELN); MLIS2 (Barth — thick cortex + brainstem/cerebellar hypoplasia)
HI; severe malnutrition; fetal alcohol; CMV/Toxo/Zika; maternal PKU; ionizing radiation; anticonvulsants

blocks.text: 7.5 Megalencephaly / Hemimegalencephaly
Hemimegalencephaly: one hemisphere enlarged + dysplastic (PMG, heterotopias, WM gliosis); sporadic; associated with Proteus, Klippel-Trenaunay-Weber, Ito hypomelanosis, tuberous sclerosis
Clinical: macrocrania, hemiplegia, severe/intractable epilepsy, developmental delay; management: hemispherectomy in refractory epilepsy
Symmetric megalencephaly: may be familial (AD); some normal outcome if isolated; distinguish from external hydrocephalus (↑ pericerebral spaces, usually excellent prognosis)
8. Disorders — Neuronal Migration: Lissencephalies
8.1 Type I (Classic) Lissencephalies

Type I Lissencephaly — Clinical Summary
Cortex: 5–20 mm (normal 2.5–4 mm); figure-of-eight on axial MRI (shallow Sylvian fissures); colpocephaly. Incidence: 1.2/100,000 live births. OFC > −3 SD by definition. LIS1 mutations (40%): posteroanterior gradient; grades 2–3; 17p13 deletion → Miller-Dieker syndrome (dysmorphic facies). DCX mutations (40%): X-linked; boys = lissencephaly (rostrocaudal gradient); girls = 'double cortex' (subcortical laminar heterotopia) → variable epilepsy; normal MRI in carrier ≠ no mutation. Tubulin (TUBA1A, TUBB2B, TUBB3): variable lissencephaly ± CC agenesis + brainstem/cerebellar hypoplasia. ARX (X-linked): lissencephaly ± CC agenesis; XLAG syndrome (lissencephaly + ambiguous genitalia + seizures beginning prenatally). Wide ARX spectrum: from X-linked ID → severe lissencephaly.
8.2 Lissencephaly With Cerebellar Hypoplasia (LCH)
Type A LCH: LIS1 or DCX mutations; + vermis/cerebellar hemisphere hypoplasia
Type B LCH: RELN mutations (autosomal recessive); more severe; rostrocaudal gradient similar to DCX; cortex 5–10 mm
Other types: involve CC agenesis, DW malformation, cleft palate, MOPD-like
8.3 Type II (Cobblestone) Lissencephalies

Type II Cobblestone Lissencephaly — O-Glycosylation Defects
Mechanism: defective O-glycosylation of alpha-dystroglycan → defective glial-limiting membrane → neurons migrate TOO FAR into subpial space → bumpy 'cobblestone' appearance. Incidence ~1/100,000. Walker-Warburg (WWS) — POMT1/POMT2: most severe; HARD±E (Hydrocephalus, Agyria, Retinal Dysplasia ± Encephalocele); eye abnormalities (cataracts, microphthalmia, retinal dysplasia); muscular dystrophy; die within first months. MEB (muscle-eye-brain) — POMGnT1: congenital myopia + glaucoma + retinal hypoplasia; pachygyria; flat brainstem; MR. Fukuyama — FKTN gene: milder brain than WWS; eyes occasionally affected; muscular dystrophy. Additional genes: FKRP, POMGNT2, DAG1, ISPD, LAMB1.
8.4 Periventricular Heterotopia (X-Linked)
FLNA gene (filamin A — actin-binding phosphoprotein): X-linked dominant; neuronal nodules lining ventricular surface
Males: lethal in embryo; females: epilepsy; also PDA, coagulopathy (loss-of-function → extraneural abnormalities)
Normal MRI in carrier female does not exclude mutation
8.5 Zellweger Syndrome (Peroxisome Biogenesis Disorder)
Absent functional peroxisomes (PEX gene mutations; autosomal recessive): heterotopic neurons in neocortex + cerebellum + inferior olivary complex
Presentation: severe psychomotor retardation/regression, facial dysmorphism, severe hypotonia; die within first postnatal months
Mechanism: PAF synthesis deficit → NMDA receptor dysfunction → abnormal calcium mobilization → migration defect
8.6 Environmental Causes of Migration Disorders
CMV, Toxoplasma, rubella, Zika: neuronal migration disorders + polymicrogyria + heterotopia
Ethanol: disturbs radial glia + RGC→astrocyte transformation; molecular ectopias in human FAS; blocks NMDA, activates GABA-A
Cocaine: ↓ GABA interneuron migration from ganglionic eminence specifically; cortical addressing disrupted
Ionizing radiation: migration disorder in animal models
9. NICU Practical — Protecting the Developing Brain
9.1 MRI สำหรับ Brain Maturation Assessment
Conventional MRI sequences: T1 (lipid/early myelination = high signal); T2 (water/unmyelinated = high signal); T1 shortening precedes T2 shortening anatomically

DTI (diffusion tensor imaging): FA (fractional anisotropy) ↑ with maturation; radial organization of cortex at 26 wk (radial glia) → declines 36 wk; NODDI distinguishes WM vs GM; interhemispheric callosal fibers visible at 28 wk
1H-MRS: ↑ NAA GW 32–40 (pre-myelination lipid synthesis); ↑ Cho (choline = cell membrane); ↓ myoinositol first year; ↑ Cr with maturation; Glx present
Sulcation index: derived from 3D cortical surface reconstruction; preterm at term equivalent → lower sulcation vs term controls → early marker of disrupted development (Dubois 2008)
Low-intensity periventricular T2 bands: normal in <30 wk (migrating RGCs, not WMI); must distinguish from PVL which shows loss of periventricular volume + cysts or diffuse signal abnormality
9.2 Protecting the Developing Brain in NICU
Minimize NMDA blockers + GABA-A agonists: use lowest effective doses of phenobarbital, ketamine, benzodiazepines; document total exposure; justify each course of steroids
Optimize oxygenation: hypoxia → HI → ↑ apoptosis + preoligodendrocyte death; hyperoxia → ROS → ↑ preoligodendrocyte vulnerability
Kangaroo care: ↓ HPA axis damage from painful procedures (Mooney-Leber & Brummelte Neuroscience 2017); ↑ autonomic maturation, ↑ sleep quality — directly protects developing synapses
Protect subplate neurons: prevent PVL — avoid hemodynamic instability, infection, hypoglycemia; control hypoxia → preserve thalamo-cortical connectivity

Sensory environment: developmentally appropriate stimulation (NIDCAP); shield from excessive noise/light in 2nd-3rd trimester equivalent ages; auditory enrichment (parental voice, music) during synaptogenesis window
Thyroid function: thyroid hormones are key modulators of neuronal migration + myelination; screen + treat hypothyroidism (including transient in very preterm)
9.3 Neural Tube Defect — Delivery Room Protocol
Myelomeningocele at birth: cover defect with sterile moist gauze; prone or lateral decubitus; avoid pressure on sac
Assess: level of defect, presence of hydrocephalus (OFC, fontanelle, US), lower limb movement/sensation, sphincter tone
Surgical consultation immediately: closure target <24h; discuss with family before surgery: realistic functional prognosis based on lesion level
Post-op monitoring: head circumference daily; rising OFC → shunt evaluation; UTI prophylaxis ongoing
10. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — Preoligodendrocytes: Window of Maximum Vulnerability GW 23–32
Preoligodendrocytes (O4+/O1−) เป็น dominant cell type ใน periventricular white matter ช่วง GW 23–32 และ vulnerable ที่สุดต่อ: (1) oxidative stress, (2) AMPA-kainate receptor excitotoxicity, (3) NMDA-mediated injury, (4) HI. นี่คือ mechanistic basis ของ PVL ใน VLBW. Up to 50% OLs ตาย ด้วย physiologic apoptosis ระหว่าง development — injury เพิ่มเติมทับซ้อน → ↓ myelination → CP/cognitive sequelae. เป้าหมาย: ป้องกัน hypoxia, infection, hemodynamic instability ช่วงนี้

PEARL 2 — Phenobarbital และ Ketamine: Exacerbate Physiologic Apoptosis
Drugs ที่เราใช้บ่อยที่สุดในการรักษาชีวิต — phenobarbital, benzos, ketamine — เป็น NMDA blockers และ/หรือ GABA-A agonists → block neuronal electrical activity → exacerbate physiologic apoptosis ใน developing brain (Olney 2002). Dexamethasone postnatal: ↓ cortical gray matter volume on MRI (Murphy Pediatrics 2001) → ↑ CP + cognitive impairment. หลักการ: ใช้เฉพาะเมื่อ benefit > risk; document total dose; ไม่ใช้ steroids สำหรับ CLD เว้นแต่ respiratory failure; prefer lower-dose regimens.

PEARL 3 — ลืม 'Inside-Out' ไม่ได้: Cortical Layers สร้างจากล่างขึ้นบน
Cortical neurons migrate inside-out: layer VI สร้างก่อน, layer II สร้างทีหลังสุด (เกิดทีหลัง → migrate ผ่านชั้นเก่าขึ้นไปอยู่ชั้นบน). ดังนั้น migration disorders ส่งผลต่อ layer II–III มากที่สุด (เพราะ neurons เกิดล่าสุด = migrate ไกลที่สุด ผ่าน layers เก่าทั้งหมด). LIS1 mutations: posteroanterior gradient (posterior worse); DCX mutations: rostrocaudal gradient (frontal worse). Knowing gradient = knowing gene = guiding genetics workup.

PEARL 4 — Subplate Neurons: Preterm Brain's Most Vulnerable Relay Station
Subplate neurons present GW 22–36 wk; absent in term brain. หน้าที่: (1) guide axons ของ internal capsule; (2) corpus callosum axons GW 25–32; (3) waiting zone สำหรับ thalamo-cortical projections ก่อน layer IV forms. PVL ใน preterm → destroys subplate → disrupts thalamo-cortical connectivity → sensory processing + cognitive deficits even without cortical injury. นี่คือเหตุที่ VLBW infants มี neurodevelopmental sequelae แม้จะไม่มี IVH หรือ cystic PVL บน MRI.

PEARL 5 — Cobblestone Lissencephaly: Walker-Warburg → Die Within Months
Walker-Warburg syndrome (POMT1/POMT2 mutations) = most severe form of type II cobblestone lissencephaly. HARD±E syndrome: Hydrocephalus + Agyria + Retinal Dysplasia ± Encephalocele + muscular dystrophy. Mechanism: defective O-glycosylation of alpha-dystroglycan → defective glial-limiting membrane → neurons over-migrate into subpial space. Prognosis: die within first postnatal months. MEB (POMGnT1) และ Fukuyama (FKTN) are milder. Key genetic test: alpha-dystroglycan immunostaining + next-gen panel (POMT1/2, POMGnT1, FKTN, FKRP, DAG1).
11. Evidence Update — Key Studies
Rakic P Science 1988;241:170: specification of cerebral cortical areas; radial unit hypothesis; protomap in germinative zone; foundational for understanding cortical topography
Sidman RL, Rakic P Brain Res 1973;62:1: neuronal migration with special reference to developing human brain — seminal review establishing migration timeline GW 12–24
LaMonica BE et al Curr Opin Neurobiol 2012;22:747: OSVZ progenitors in human cortex (bRGC) — updated perspective; key to understanding human cortical expansion vs mice
McKinstry RC et al Cereb Cortex 2002;12:1237: DTI of preterm cortex 26–41 wk: radial organization at 26 wk (radial glia) → decline to 0 by 36 wk; vector maps of cortical development in vivo
Hüppi PS et al Ann Neurol 1998;43:224: quantitative MRI brain development premature/mature newborns: brain volume doubles, cortical GM ↑ 4-fold, cortical surface ↑ 5-fold between 28–40 wk

Dubois J et al Cereb Cortex 2008;18:1444: mapping early cortical folding in preterm brain; sulcation index; central region folds first; females have smaller volumes but equivalent sulcation; prematurity disrupts normal sulcal development
Back SA et al J Neurosci 2001;21:1302: late OL progenitors (preoligodendrocytes) coincide with developmental vulnerability window for human perinatal WM injury — established GW 23–32 vulnerability
Volpe JJ Pediatr Res 2001;50:553: neurobiology of periventricular leukomalacia in premature infant — definitive review linking OL vulnerability, excitotoxicity, oxidative stress to WMI

Olney JW et al Brain Pathol 2002;12:488: drug-induced apoptotic neurodegeneration in developing brain: NMDA blockers + GABA-A agonists → exacerbate physiologic apoptosis; direct clinical relevance for NICU pharmacology
Murphy BP et al Pediatrics 2001;107:217: postnatal dexamethasone for neonatal CLD → marked reduction in cortical GM volume on MRI → mechanistic link to ↑ CP + cognitive impairment in follow-up
Changeux JP, Danchin A Nature 1976;264:705: selective stabilization of developing synapses — epigenetic hypothesis; labile synapses overproduced, activity-dependent elimination shapes final network
Tremblay ME Neuron Glia Biol 2011;7:67: microglia role at synapses in healthy CNS: quiescent microglia extend processes + phagocytose terminals/spines = active synaptic pruning in normal development
Mooney-Leber SM, Brummelte S Neuroscience 2017;356:177: kangaroo care negates HPA axis damage from painful NICU procedures — direct mechanistic evidence for KMC protecting developing stress circuitry
Dobyns WB, Truwit CL Neuropediatrics 1995;26:132: lissencephaly radiologic severity grading system (grades 1–6); posteroanterior vs rostrocaudal gradient → predicts LIS1 vs DCX mutation
Inder TE et al Pediatrics 2005;115:286: abnormal cerebral structure at term in premature infants: ↓ cortical volume + surface, less complex sulcation vs term-born; ↑ effect with lower GA
Kreis R et al Magn Reson Med 2002;48:949: quantitative 1H-MRS: NAA markedly ↑ GW 32–40 in central WM (prelipid synthesis for myelination); stable in perithalamic voxels
Kingler E et al Science 2021;371:361: mapping molecular and cellular complexity of cortical malformations — comprehensive modern review of malformation genetics and mechanisms
Zaqout S, Kaindl AM Front Cell Dev Biol 2022;9:784700: autosomal recessive primary microcephaly — not just a small brain; comprehensive review of MCPH gene functions in cell-cycle control
12. Self-Check Q&A
Q: Neural tube closure เกิดเมื่อไหร่ และ failure ของ anterior vs posterior neuropore → disorder ชนิดใด?

ตอบ: Anterior neuropore closes ~day 24: failure → anencephaly. Posterior neuropore closes ~day 26 (lumbosacral level): failure → myeloschisis + myelomeningocele. Encephalocele = restricted anterior closure failure <26 days. Craniorachischisis = total failure ≤22 days. Neural groove forms GW 3; tube closes beginning GW 4 from lower medulla both caudally and rostrally.
Q: อธิบาย inside-out pattern ของ cortical lamination และความสำคัญต่อ migration disorders

ตอบ: Inside-out: cortical neurons form layer VI first (earliest neurons), then V, IV, III, II — neurons born later must migrate PAST earlier neurons. Clinical significance: neurons born latest (layer II–III) travel farthest → most vulnerable to migration defects. LIS1 mutations → posteroanterior gradient (posterior cortex = later myelination → more severe); DCX mutations → rostrocaudal gradient (frontal worse). Understanding gradient → predicts causative gene.
Q: Preoligodendrocytes: ทำไมถึง vulnerable และ clinical relevance ต่อ PVL?

ตอบ: Preoligodendrocytes (O4+/O1−): dominant cell in periventricular WM at GW 23–32. Vulnerable to: (1) oxidative stress (↓ antioxidant enzymes); (2) AMPA-kainate excitotoxicity; (3) NMDA activation; (4) HI. PVL mechanism: preterm birth within this window → injury to preoligodendrocytes → ↓ myelination → CP + cognitive sequelae even without overt cystic PVL. Up to 50% of normally produced OLs die by apoptosis — additional injury amplifies this loss dramatically.
Q: จงบอก LIS1 mutations vs DCX mutations: gradient, เพศ, ความหนา cortex, syndrome

ตอบ: LIS1 (PAFAH1B1, 40% of lissencephaly): posteroanterior gradient; cortex 10–20 mm; autosomal dominant; 17p13 deletion → Miller-Dieker syndrome (grade 1 agyria + dysmorphic facies). DCX (X-linked, 40%): rostrocaudal gradient; boys = lissencephaly (grade 1–4); girls = subcortical laminar heterotopia ('double cortex') — band of gray matter between cortex and lateral ventricles; variable epilepsy; normal MRI in carrier ≠ no mutation. Both → thickened cortex (10–20 mm).
Q: Walker-Warburg vs MEB vs Fukuyama — เหมือนกันอย่างไร ต่างกันตรงไหน?

ตอบ: Shared mechanism: O-glycosylation defect of alpha-dystroglycan → defective glial-limiting membrane → cobblestone type II lissencephaly. WWS (POMT1/POMT2): most severe; HARD±E; lethal within months; severe eye involvement. MEB (POMGnT1): congenital myopia + glaucoma + retinal hypoplasia; pachygyria + flat brainstem; cognitive impairment. Fukuyama (FKTN): mildest brain; eyes occasionally affected; Japanese predominance; most patients survive longer.
Q: Subplate neurons: เกิดเมื่อไหร่ ทำหน้าที่อะไร และทำไมถึงสำคัญต่อ preterm brain?

ตอบ: Generated ~GW 7; maximal GW 22–36; disappear by term. Functions: (1) pioneer axons for internal capsule; (2) CC axons GW 25–32; (3) waiting zone for thalamo-cortical afferents (before layer IV forms → correct target selection). PVL destroys subplate: disrupts thalamo-cortical connectivity → sensory processing deficits + cognitive impairment even without cortical injury — explains neurodevelopmental sequelae in VLBW with diffuse WMI but no obvious cortical lesion on MRI.
Q: Programmed cell death ใน brain development: ทำไม drugs อย่าง phenobarbital ถึงเป็นปัญหา?

ตอบ: Physiologic apoptosis: 15–50% neurons eliminated; peak GW 28–41; requires caspase cascade activation (intrinsic Bcl-2 pathway + extrinsic death receptors). Electrical activity = key survival signal: drugs that BLOCK electrical activity → ↑ apoptosis. Phenobarbital/benzos: GABA-A agonists → suppress activity. Ketamine: NMDA blocker → same effect. Clinical: use lowest effective dose; document total exposure; postnatal dexamethasone → ↓ cortical GM volume (Murphy 2001) → ↑ CP in follow-up → justify each course carefully.
Q: Myelination sequence: ลำดับในสมอง และ MRI changes ที่บอก progression?

ตอบ: Sequence: sensory > motor; subcortical > cortical; olfactory/optic/acoustic/sensorimotor first; projection fibers before association; association fibers complete in adulthood. Cerebellar pedunculi + vermis before hemispheres. T1 shortening (lipid accumulation) PRECEDES T2 shortening (myelin tightening): T1 detects 'pre-myelination'; T2 detects 'mature myelin'. VPL thalamus: T1 at 28–30 wk. Posterior PLIC: T1 at 38–40 wk, T2 at term. Myelinated WM <5% brain volume at 34 wk → rapid 5-fold increase postnatally.
Q: Holoprosencephaly: types, most important gene, neonatal signs

ตอบ: Alobar (absent interhemispheric fissure, CC, 3rd ventricle, single ventricle) → semilobar (caudal IHF present) → lobar (3rd ventricle + CC present, frontal hypoplasia). All have absent septum pellucidum + trigone + hypoplastic olfactory bulbs. Most important gene: SHH (HPE3); also ZIC2, SIX3, TGIF. Maternal diabetes + ethanol are environmental causes. Neonatal signs: apneas, seizures, tonic spasms, poor neurologic development; dysmorphic facies (does not always correlate with brain severity).
Q: X-linked periventricular heterotopia: gene, sex difference, neonatal management

ตอบ: FLNA gene (filamin A — actin-binding phosphoprotein): X-linked dominant. Hemizygous males: lethal in embryo. Affected females: epilepsy (variable onset/severity) + PDA + coagulopathy. MRI: neuronal nodules lining ventricular wall (periventricular). Normal MRI does NOT exclude carrier status. Management: antiepileptic therapy; genetic counseling; prenatal diagnosis available (FLNA mutation screening). Loss-of-function mechanism = loss of FilaminA → impaired neuronal locomotion.
Q: Kangaroo care กับ HPA axis: mechanism ที่ protect developing brain

ตอบ: Painful procedures (heel stick, IV, NGT) → ↑ glucocorticoid release → alter HPA axis programming → ↓ cortisol response to future stress → ↑ adverse behavioral outcomes. Kangaroo care negates this (Mooney-Leber & Brummelte Neuroscience 2017): maternal KMC → sensory triad (touch/warmth/odor) → oxytocin release → buffer stress response + ↓ glucocorticoid surge. Additionally: ↑ quiet sleep + longer sleep cycles → promotes synaptogenesis. Practical: KMC during heel stick = analgesic (viable ≥28 wk GA) + neuroprotective simultaneously.

Day 151 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.54
บทต่อไป: Chapter 54 — Cerebral White Matter Damage and Encephalopathy of Prematurity. เตรียมตัว: periventricular leukomalacia (PVL) — focal cystic + diffuse non-cystic forms; pathogenesis (preoligodendrocyte vulnerability, excitotoxicity, oxidative stress, inflammation cascade); germinal matrix-IVH: grading (Papile I–IV), pathophysiology, complications (PHVD, post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus); neuroimaging — cranial US vs MRI at term equivalent age; neurodevelopmental outcomes: CP types, cognitive, visual, behavioral sequelae; neuroprotective strategies: antenatal MgSO4, indomethacin prophylaxis, delayed cord clamping, hemodynamic optimization.
